text: Robotic approach to delayed anastomotic leak after ileocecectomy for a neuroendocrine tumor.
Robotic approach to delayed anastomotic leak after ileocecectomy for a neuroendocrine tumor.
A sixty-seven-year-old female presents with a five-day history of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
A sixty-seven-year-old female presents with a five-day history of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
She has a history of an ileocolic resection six weeks ago.

The patient had a postoperative course complicated by a prolonged ileus.

She is afebrile with a heart rate of ninety-three and a white blood cell count of eleven point eight.

Patient presented to an outside institution six weeks ago with an asymptomatic right lower quadrant mass.

She underwent a diagnostic laparoscopy, was found to have an ileal tumor, subsequently converted to an open ileocolic resection with a stapled side-to-side anastomosis.

The final pathology showed a two-centimeter low-grade neuroendocrine tumor, which extended through the muscularis propia into the mesenteric lymph nodes involved by carcinide.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach to evaluate the anastomosis, encountering visible adhesions in the surgical field.
The robotic surgical system is used to evaluate the anastomosis site, revealing visible adhesions surrounding the area.
Four surgical ports are placed vertically in the left hemiabdomen. Upon entry, dense adhesions are visible within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon sharply dissects and removes the dense adhesions observed in the left hemiabdomen.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to take down the dense adhesions in the left hemiabdomen.
The surgeon identifies a perforation at the site of the previous anastomosis, visible within the surgical field.
The surgeon observes a perforation at the site of the prior anastomosis, with visible disruption in the tissue continuity.
Dense adhesions are visible in the surgical field during the robotic evaluation of the anastomosis in the left hemiabdomen.
Dense adhesions are visible from the colon to the liver and the abdominal sidewall. The surgeon sharply dissects these adhesions to separate the structures.
The surgeon sharply dissects adhesions extending from the colon to the liver and the abdominal sidewall, using precise techniques to separate these structures.
The surgeon sharply dissects dense interloop adhesions within the abdominal cavity, separating the loops of bowel from each other.
The surgeon uses sharp dissection to take down dense interloop adhesions within the abdominal cavity, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The video shows significant inflammation in the surgical field, with the bowel visibly adherent to the mesentery, indicating dense adhesions.
The video shows significant inflammation with the bowel tightly adherent to the mesentery, indicating dense adhesions in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses a sponge to dissect through the interloop adhesions, carefully separating the bowel loops from each other.

During the dissection of interloop adhesions, a small serosal injury is observed on the bowel surface.

The surgeon sutures the serosal injury using interrupted 3-0 Vicryl stitches, carefully closing the defect in the bowel wall.
The surgeon sutures the serosal injury using interrupted 3-0 Vicryl sutures, carefully closing the defect in the bowel wall.
The surgeon creates a small window in the mesentery after dissecting the terminal ileum free from adhesions, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon creates a small window in the mesentery after dissecting the terminal ileum free from dense interloop adhesions.
The surgeon controls bleeding using bipolar forceps and a vessel sealer, which is then used to divide the small bowel mesentery, separating it from the adherent bowel.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the small bowel, creating an adequate mesenteric window. A 45mm stapler is then applied to divide the terminal ileum.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm to retract the small bowel, creating an adequate mesenteric window. A 45mm stapler is then employed to divide the terminal ileum, with the retraction providing clear visualization of the procedure.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the remaining small bowel mesentery, progressing down to the level of the previous anastomosis.

The surgeon observes the colon distal to the anastomosis, noting its adherence to the liver and adjacent small bowel loops.
The surgeon observes the colon distal to the anastomosis, noting its adherence to the liver and surrounding small bowel loops.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection using a sponge and suctioning to separate tissue layers, focusing on the mesenteric area.
The surgeon performs sharp dissection using a sponge and suctioning to separate tissue layers, focusing on the area around the terminal ileum and mesentery.
The surgeon sharply dissects the hepatic flexure and transverse colon away from the edge of the liver, using a sponge and the fourth arm to retract the liver for better exposure.

The surgeon creates a window in the colonic mesentery five centimeters from the previous anastomosis. The colon is elevated and divided using a 45mm stapler, visibly separating the tissue.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the remaining colonic mesentery, while sharply dissecting any adhesions between the colon and mesocolon.

The surgeon frees the prior anastomosis from the mesocolon and positions it aside, revealing the surrounding tissue and anatomical structures.
The surgeon frees the prior anastomosis from the mesocolon and places it aside, clearing the surgical field for further dissection.
The surgeon sharply lyses any remaining adhesions in the surgical field, ensuring clear separation of the tissues.
The surgeon sharply lyses any remaining adhesions between the colon and surrounding tissues, ensuring clear separation.

The surgeon flips the terminal ileum and positions it in the right upper quadrant alongside the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, which is retracted using the fourth arm. Monopolar scissors are used to create a colotomy and enterotomy.

The terminal ileum is flipped and positioned in the right upper quadrant alongside the transverse colon. A stay suture is placed to stabilize the anastomosis, which is retracted using the fourth arm. The surgeon creates a colotomy and enterotomy using monopolar scissors.
The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 45mm GIA stapler, aligning the bowel segments side by side for stapling.
The surgeon creates an isoparastaltic side-to-side anastomosis using a 45mm GIA stapler, joining the bowel segments in parallel alignment.
The surgeon removes the stay suture that was previously placed to stabilize the anastomosis in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon closes the common channel using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, employing a canal fashion technique to secure the closure.
The surgeon closes the common channel using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture, employing a canal fashion technique to secure the tissue.
The surgeon optimizes exposure and retraction of the surgical field using the fourth arm, enhancing visibility and access to the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon optimizes exposure and retraction of the surgical field using the fourth arm, enhancing visibility of the terminal ileum and transverse colon during the procedure.
Indocyanine green (ICG) dye is applied to assess the bowel perfusion and blood supply of the transverse colon and ileum, highlighting vascular flow in these areas.

The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a running fashion, ensuring the defect is securely sealed.
The surgeon closes the mesenteric defect using a 3-0 absorbable barbed suture in a running fashion, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgical specimen is placed into a retrieval bag and extracted through the stapler port.
The surgeon places the resected specimen into a retrieval bag and extracts it through the stapler port.

Final pathology showed a one millimeter incidental low-grade neuroendocrine tumor near the anastomotic junction at the muscularis propria, four reactive lymph nodes, and the anastomosis with ulceration and perforation.

Final pathology showed a one millimeter incidental low-grade neuroendocrine tumor near the anastomotic junction at the muscularis propria, four reactive lymph nodes, and the anastomosis with ulceration and perforation.
The patient's post-operative course was uncomplicated, and she was discharged on post-op day number five.
The patient's post-operative course was uncomplicated, and she was discharged on post-op day number five.

She underwent outpatient oncology follow-up with a Gallium-XXVIII dotatate scan, which was negative, found to have an elevated chromogranin A. She was started on octreotide therapy for significant flushing, and at her one-year follow-up, a repeat CT abdomen and pelvis showed no recurrence.
